coredata.dc:title: Preparation and characterization of a CDs based hydrogel slow-release fertilizer
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Hong
An, Zehua
Zhang, Haoran
Li, Wei
Yang, Xian
Kang, Yunyan
Su, Wei
Zheng, Mingtao
Lei, Bingfu
coredata.dc:description: Low utilization rate of chemical fertilizers is a main limitation for improving crop yields. Peanut bran carbon dots (CDs) have been shown to boost Italian lettuce growth, while hydrophilic CDs can easily flow away with water during application, which leads to CDs wastage and water losing. Therefore, it is crucial to develop a material with slow-release capacity of nutrient elements and CDs along with water-retaining function. In this study, acrylic acid and acrylamide were reacted with urea, potassium phosphate and other materials, thus a hydrogel slow-release fertilizer was firstly designed. Then, peanut bran CDs were added into the hydrogels to prepare a novel CDs based hydrogel slow-release fertilizer (CDs-hydrogels). Experimental results show that the prepared CDs-hydrogels have high water retention capacity and element release capacity of N, P, K. Most importantly, the CDs within these hydrogels, via its changes in luminescence, the releasing rate of nutrient elements can be monitored.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Luminescence
Fertilizer
Carbon dots
Optical-agriculture

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: N, P, K release performance of CD-hydrogels
Water absorption and retention capacity of CDs-hydrogels

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work was supported by the Guangdong Provincial Special Fund for Modern Agriculture Industry Technology Innovation Teams (No. 2023KJ122 ), the Key Realm R&D Program of Guangdong Province (No. 2021B0707010003 ), the Guangzhou Science & Technology Project (No. 202103000059 ), and the Project of GDUPS (2018) for Prof. Bingfu LEI.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis process of CDs-hydrogels.

(a) SEM images of CDs-hydrogels. (b) Elemental mapping images for N, K, P within the as-prepared CDs-hydrogels. (c) Thermogravimetric curves for CDs-hydrogels. (d) XRD patterns of CDs-hydrogels, CH4N2O, K3PO4 and K2HPO4. (e) The contact angle for biochar and CDs-hydrogels.
(a) FTIR spectra of CDs-hydrogels and raw materials. (b) XPS patterns of CDs. (c − e) High-resolution XPS patterns of the C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s of CDs-hydrogels, respectively.
(a) Water absorption property of CDs-hydrogels. (b) Water retention capacity of CDs-hydrogels.
Fertilizer release properties of CDs-hydrogels in water: (a) N release; (b) P release; (c) K release.
PL spectra of sustained-release solutions of different concentrations of CDs-hydrogels.

head.ce:title: Preparation and characterization of a CDs based hydrogel slow-release fertilizer

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Biobased Materials and Energy of Ministry of Education

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Horticulture, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou 510642, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Low utilization rate of chemical fertilizers is a main limitation for improving crop yields. Peanut bran carbon dots (CDs) have been shown to boost Italian lettuce growth, while hydrophilic CDs can easily flow away with water during application, which leads to CDs wastage and water losing. Therefore, it is crucial to develop a material with slow-release capacity of nutrient elements and CDs along with water-retaining function. In this study, acrylic acid and acrylamide were reacted with urea, potassium phosphate and other materials, thus a hydrogel slow-release fertilizer was firstly designed. Then, peanut bran CDs were added into the hydrogels to prepare a novel CDs based hydrogel slow-release fertilizer (CDs-hydrogels). Experimental results show that the prepared CDs-hydrogels have high water retention capacity and element release capacity of N, P, K. Most importantly, the CDs within these hydrogels, via its changes in luminescence, the releasing rate of nutrient elements can be monitored.

ce:list-item.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: As an important role in modern agriculture, chemical fertilizers have become an indispensable product for farmers today. Applying chemical fertilizers is the most effective way to increase crop yields, thus people will be unable to get rid of their dependence on chemical fertilizers in a long period [1]. However, the utilization rate of chemical fertilizers remains low in many countries at present. Such as the utilization rate of potash fertilizer is only 35 %, which of nitrogen fertilizer is only 30 %, and that of phosphate fertilizer even goes down to only 10 % to 25 % [2]. The low utilization rate is mostly led by fertilizer losing in soil, it not only causes a serious waste of resources, but also keeps a large amount of chemical fertilizer accumulating in the soil, which leads to the destruction of farmland and the environment, along with reducing quality of crops [3–6]. Therefore, increasing the utilization rate of chemical fertilizers is of great significance to the development of agriculture and the protection of the environment.

In recent years, a growing number of researches were done on carbon dots (CDs). CDs attract many researchers’ attention due to their unique properties such as inherent photoluminescence properties, excellent photostability, small size (less than 10 nm), high biocompatibility, water solubility, highly tunable PL properties, easy functionalizing with biomolecules, and chemical inertness [7–9]. With recent developments, some of these CDs have been developed for agricultural applications [10–13], such as CDs that can be used for monitoring crop health and promoting crop growth [14]. On the other hand, some studies have also shown that applying high concentrations of CDs inhibits plant growth instead of promoting it [15]. At the same time, unabsorbed CDs will flow into the ground with water due to the excellent water solubility, which causes waste. Therefore, it is very important to develop a carrier material that can release CDs slowly.

Water is also an indispensable factor in plant growth, which acts as a solvent for many nutrient elements in soil while regulating plant temperature. In arid or semi-arid areas without sufficient water, it is especially important to maintain soil moisture and avoid water losing [16]. So there is a need for developing fertilizers with functions of water retaining.

To solve the problem of massive waste on CDs and fertilizers as well as water losing, it is proposed to prepare a hydrogel slow-release water-retaining fertilizer. This type of compound transports fertilizer and CDs to plants in a slower way, so that it can better meet the nutrient needs of crops. At the same time, its water retaining capability also reduces water losing. In this study, acrylamide acrylate was used as raw material for hydrogel preparation. Acrylic monomers, such as acrylic, methacrylic acid and acrylamide exhibit high reactivity in free-radical polymerization, implying that these compounds can form hydrophilic polymers. Then, peanut bran derived CDs were incorporated into the hydrogels [17]. Besides, peanut bran derived CDs also improve the slow-release ability as it has abundant surface functional groups which are capable of absorbing nutrients [18–20]. Then a graft copolymerization in a certain proportion was carried out to produce the CDs based water-retaining slow-release fertilizer. In addition, bentonite was added to the hydrogel system. Low-cost bentonite can increase the solid content and improve the mechanical strength of the hydrogels [21]. Furthermore, the application of peanut bran derived CDs have been proven to promote Italian lettuce growing and act as a fine carbon fertilizer [22]. Peanut bran derived CDs also improved the fertilizer properties of hydrogel slow-release fertilizers. The luminescent properties of the CDs component provide its function that as luminescent labels to monitor the nutriment elements that absorbed by the plants.

Peanut bran and bentonite were purchased from Taobao, China. Acrylic acid, acrylamide, urea, potassium phosphate, N,N-methylenebisacrylamide (MBA), potassium hydroxide, and potassium persulfate (KPS) were bought from China Shanghai Aladdin Chemical Co, Ltd. These chemical reagents are of analytical grade, and there is no further purification before use. All experiments are done with deionized water.

The CDs were prepared by the carbonization method, using peanut bran as the raw material [22]. Peanut bran was pulverized and heated in a tube furnace under the protection of nitrogen. The reaction temperature was kept at 270 °C for 1 h. Then the system was naturally cooled to room temperature to obtain a pack of black solid, which was then ground into powder. The black powder was put into deionized water, stirred, and ultrasonically treated for 30 min. The mixed solution was filtered with a 0.22 μm filter membrane to remove insoluble solids. The filtrate was dialyzed with a 3000 Da dialysis bag for 24 h to filter out the small molecules. The obtained yellow-brown solution was an aqueous solution of peanut bran CDs, which was reserved for future applications.

The preparation process of CDs-hydrogels was follows. First, 2 g of KOH was dissolved in 30 mL of deionized water in a 500 mL three-necked flask with stirring and maintain a constant temperature of 70 °C in water bath. Then, 5 mL of acrylic acid (AA), 3 g of acrylamide (AM), 2 g of urea granules, 0.3 g of K3PO4 and 0.07 g of MBA were added into the mixed solution and stirred for 15 min. Finally, 1 g of peanut bran biochar, 0.3 g of bentonite, 0.15 g of KPS, and 30 mL of CDs solution (1 mg/mL) were added to the three-necked flask. The water bath method was kept at 70 °C and the stirring was continued for 4 h until the polyreaction was complete, and the entire reaction was protected by nitrogen. After the reaction, the product was washed with excess absolute ethanol to remove the unreacted raw materials. Finally, the CDs-hydrogels were cut into small pieces and placed in a drying oven at 70 °C for not less than 48 h until the weight stayed unchanged, then the final product was obtained. After the experiment, the product was stored in a sealed bag for later use. The specific synthesis process of CDs-hydrogels is shown in Fig. 1 .

The surface morphology of the as-prepared CDs-hydrogels was investigated by scanning electron microscopy using a Hitachi Regulus 8100 instrument. The surface groups of the raw materials and CDs-hydrogels were observed by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy and XPS using Nicolet iS 10 and Thermo Kalpha. JC2000–2C was used to contact angle analyzer to analyze the surface energy and polar force. The XRD data of the starting materials and products were analyzed by X-ray diffractometer BrukerAXS. The luminescence intensity of CDs in the slow-release solution of CDs was measured by Hitachi F-7000 spectrometer.

The swelling properties of CDs-hydrogels were explored by studying its swelling rate over time. First, CDs-hydrogels of equal mass were respectively immersed in the same volume of deionized water (pH = 7) at room temperature (25 °C). Then the swollen CDs-hydrogels were extracted at the same time interval, and the excess water on the surface was absorbed with filter paper. The weights of CDs-hydrogels were weighed. The operations above continued until CDs-hydrogels reaches a swelling balance and the experiment was stopped when the quality of CDs-hydrogels doesn't increase anymore. All swelling experiments were performed three times, and the average value was taken. The SR value can be calculated by the formula [23]:
(1)
SR
(
g / g
)
=
M t
−
M 0
M 0

Two mixed samples were taken: I: 150 g of vermiculite, II: 149 g of vermiculite and 1 g of CDs-hydrogels. The mixed samples were placed in three 500 mL beakers, followed by 200 mL of deionized water. The beakers were kept standing for 24 h, then excess water was poured off, the systems were weighed again. After that, we weighed every 24 h and kept the same humidity throughout the experimental environment. In this experiment, CDs-hydrogels was applied in the experimental group, and no CDs-hydrogel was added in the control group. The initial total weight was kept the same.

ce:section.ce:section-title: N, P, K release performance of CD-hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: A fertilizer release test of CDs-hydrogels was conducted in water. 0.2 g of CDs-hydrogels were immersed in 200 mL of 25 °C deionized water (pH = 7), and the weight was recorded. The deionized water was replaced every 12 h while maintaining the same total weight as first weighing. Operations above lasted for 84 h, and the element contents of N, P, K were tested each time before water replacement. This method was to simulate the process of plants absorbing N, P, K elements thus to test the fertilizer release performance of CDs-hydrogels. Fertilizer filling and release tests were carried out 3 times in total, and the average values were taken.

0.2 g of CDs-hydrogels with different concentrations of carbon dots were put into 200 mL of deionized water, the deionized water was replaced every 12 h, the PL intensity of the deionized water was detected. The total weight of deionized water and CDs-hydrogels were kept unchanged each time. This experimental can simulate the process of plants absorbing CDs, thus to measure the sustained CDs release properties of CDs-hydrogels.

Fig. 2 a shows the microscopic morphology of the CDs-hydrogels. It is shown that the surface was rough and some extra small holes could be observed. In addition, no particles of urea or potassium phosphate were observed on the surface of the CDs-hydrogels, which likely indicates that urea and potassium phosphate have been incorporated into the matrix of CDs-hydrogels through graft copolymerization [24]. Fig. 2b is elemental mapping images of N, K and P within the as-prepared CDs-hydrogels, which shows that N, P, K were evenly distributed in the entire structure of CDs-hydrogels. In Fig. 2c, TGA was used to study the thermal stability of CDs-hydrogels. With the increasing temperature, the TGA curve has three main thermal decomposition stages. The first stage appears at 100–200 °C, which corresponds to the decomposition of low polymers. The second stage occurs at between 200 and 450 °C, which may be caused by the decomposition of water-retaining fertilizer polymers. Finally, the third stage occurs in the 450–800 °C range, which may be caused by further decomposition of the residue. Fig. 2d shows the XRD patterns of CDs-hydrogels. We can see that CDs-hydrogels have a broad peak, indicating that it has an amorphous structure. In the XRD pattern of CDs-hydrogels, we did not find the characteristic peaks of urea and potassium phosphate, which implies that the crystal structure of urea and potassium phosphate had been destroyed. These data proved that through the graft copolymerization of biochar, urea, potassium phosphate and acrylic acid, a macromolecular polymer with an amorphous structure was formed, and all of the raw materials were successfully incorporated into CDs-hydrogels through graft copolymerization. Fig. 2e is the contact angle measurement results of peanut bran biochar and CDs-hydrogels. The results show that biochar has strong hydrophobicity (146.5°), while the contact angle of CDs-hydrogels is 22.9°, which is highly hydrophilic. A reasonable explanation is that we added bentonite to the raw materials, which could not only promote the exposure of more hydrophilic functional groups on the surface of CDs-hydrogels, but also produce a space coating that resists the hydrophobic functional groups of biochar.

Fig. 3 a shows the chemical structures of peanut bran biochar, potassium phosphate, acrylic acid, acrylamide, urea, and CDs-hydrogels in sequence. In the FTIR spectrum of peanut bran biochar, there is a broad absorption peak at 3420 cm−1, which is related to the vibration of the -OH group. For the spectrum of acrylic acid, the three peaks appearing at 1721 cm−1, 1622 cm−1, and 1412 cm−1 correspond to CO groups, -COO groups and CC groups, respectively. For the FTIR spectrum of acrylamide, two peaks at 3346 cm−1 and 1673 cm−1 can be observed, which are respectively caused by the -NH2 stretching vibration and the CO group stretching vibration peak. In the infrared spectrum of urea, two peaks can be seen at 3430 cm−1 and 3346 cm−1. These two peaks are caused by the stretching vibration of -CONH2 and -NH2, while the peak at 1673 cm−1 is caused by the CO group. As for the FTIR spectrum of CDs-hydrogels, the characteristic peaks at 3416 cm−1 and 1688 cm−1 correspond to the NH and CO stretching vibrations in -CONH2, respectively. The NH and CO vibrations may come from urea or acrylamide. The peaks at 3695 cm−1 and 1048 cm−1 correspond to -OH and COC stretching vibrations from peanut bran biochar. The peaks at 1607 cm−1 and 1445 cm−1 correspond to -COO- and -CN stretching vibration peaks. From the above results, it can be concluded that CDs-hydrogels had been successfully prepared by the graft copolymerization method.

Fig. 3 b is the XPS pattern of CDs-hydrogels. We can see that there are three peaks on CDs-hydrogels XPS spectrum, namely O 1 s (532.99 eV), N 1 s (399.31 eV) and O 1 s (285.82 eV). The C 1 s pattern in Fig. 3c can be seen to fit three peaks at the band energies of 287.65 eV, 285.62 eV, and 283.89 eV, and these three peaks represent the CO, CN, and CC groups, respectively. The N 1 s pattern in Fig. 3d can be observed to fit three peaks at the band energies of 398.48 eV, 399.71 eV, and 400.82 eV, and these three peaks represent CN-C, CN, and NH4 + groups, respectively. The O 1 s pattern in Fig. 3e can be seen to fit three peaks at the band energies of 530.29 eV, 531.91 eV, and 533.25 eV, and these three peaks represent CN, NO, and CO groups, respectively. From the XPS pattern of the CDs-hydrogels, we can see that there are abundant functional groups on the surface of the CDs-hydrogels.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water absorption and retention capacity of CDs-hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: The water absorption property and water retention capacity of CDs-hydrogels are shown in Fig. 4 . It can be seen from Fig. 4a that the CDs-hydrogels can absorb 325 % of its own weight of water, indicating that it has a good water absorption capacity. This is because CDs-hydrogels has a rich network structure, which is conducive to water absorption. FTIR spectrum and XPS patterns proved that there are hydrophilic functional groups inside, such as -OH, -CHO, -COOH and other functional groups, which can be used to absorb large amounts of water. The (Swelling Radio) SR value of the CDs-hydrogels at each moment was calculated according to formula (1). It can be seen from Fig. 4b that applying CDs-hydrogels can reduce water loss in soil.

Fig. 5 Shows the nutrient release capacity of CDs-hydrogels at 25 °C. In the early release period, CDs-hydrogels first swelled by absorbing water, and the strength of the gel structure was weakened. Part of the urea and potassium phosphate were dissolved and released gradually. At the same time, the high fertilizer concentration gradient difference between the internal of the CDs-hydrogels and the aqueous solution also caused explosive release of fertilizer [25–27]. After the initial period, the nutrient release rate slowly decreases, which mainly due to the dropping concentration difference between the inside and outside of the CDs-hydrogels [28–30]. When the water absorption reaches saturation, the strength of the gel structure was reduced to the lowest, the control of nutrients dropped to the lowest, nutrients penetrated quickly, and the release was maximized. Finally, the nutrient release is basically completed, and the release rate gradually approaches zero.

The spectral intensity of carbon dots was measured to characterize the content of carbon dots in the slow-release solution. Fig. 6 shows the CDs release ability of CDs-hydrogels containing different CDs concentrations at 25 °C. First, 0.2 g of CDs-hydrogels with different CDs concentrations were put into 200 mL of deionized water, samples were taken every 12 h to test the PL intensity, and the deionized water was replaced at the same time. The total weight of the CDs-hydrogels and deionized water were kept the same each time, and the sustained release performance of the CDs of the CDs-hydrogels was measured. This method can simulate the absorption process of plants. The luminescence intensity of CDs in the slow-release solution was measured to correspond to the content of CDs. We can see from the figure that CDs-hydrogels with different CDs concentrations can slowly release CDs. This is because we added CDs into the hydrogels by chemical cross-linking, and there were interactions between CDs and hydrogels, which could be mainly manifested in two types, hydrogen bonding, and electrostatic interaction between CDs and hydrogel surface groups [31,32]. These CDs-hydrogels, in the continuous release process of CDs, acted as the hydrogel structure which slowly dissolved, as the carbon dots inside were also released, achieving the purpose of slowly releasing CDs.

In this study, a new type of slow-release CDs-hydrogel was formed using peanut bran CDs, peanut bran biochar, bentonite, urea, potassium phosphate, acrylic acid, acrylamide, MBA, potassium hydroxide, and KPS as raw materials. After a reaction which continuously reacted for more than 4 h, a peanut bran-based water-retaining slow-release fertilizer that can slow-release three elements of N, P, and K, as well as the CDs of peanut bran, was prepared. Its morphology characteristics were observed, and the thermal stability was tested. The distribution patterns of N, P, K elements in CDs-hydrogels were also detected. Moreover, the results from contact angle measurement and XRD pattern show that CDs-hydrogels are highly hydrophilic, and the existence of an amorphous structure indicates that raw materials like urea and potassium phosphate were successfully incorporated into the hydrogel. The prepared CD-hydrogels were verified with excellent slow-release capacities on fertilizers and CDs, which meets the need of gradual and continuous nutrition supply for plants. The tests also show that the CDs-hydrogels have high level of water-absorbing property and water-retaining capacity, solving the soil issue of water losing. This study provides new ideas for the future development of water-retaining slow-release fertilizers in agriculture.

Hong Xu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Zehua An: Data curation, Software, Writing – original draft. Haoran Zhang: Visualization, Investigation. Wei Li: Resources, Supervision. Xian Yang: Resources, Supervision. Yunyan Kang: Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Wei Su: Software, Validation. Mingtao Zheng: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Bingfu Lei: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by the Guangdong Provincial Special Fund for Modern Agriculture Industry Technology Innovation Teams (No. 2023KJ122), the Key Realm R&D Program of Guangdong Province (No. 2021B0707010003), the Guangzhou Science & Technology Project (No. 202103000059), and the Project of GDUPS (2018) for Prof. Bingfu LEI.
